tool_call_sequence.description: Benchmark is L0 (diagnosis-only). Agent must correctly document the diagnosis of familial cluster headache after appropriate history and red-flag screening.

nl_assertions: Correctly identifies the condition as cluster headache and explicitly notes familial history/pattern when present.

Elicits core cluster headache features: strictly unilateral severe orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain, short duration (15–180 minutes), high attack frequency (up to 8/day), circadian/circannual periodicity, and ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms (e.g., lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, ptosis/miosis) and/or restlessness/agitation.

Screens for secondary headache red flags and alternative emergencies and adjusts plan if present: sudden thunderclap onset, new neurologic deficits, fever/meningismus, head trauma, immunosuppression/cancer, new onset after age 50, pregnancy/postpartum, jaw claudication/vision changes, or atypical/progressive pattern.

Includes an appropriate differential for unilateral autonomic headache syndromes and mimics (e.g., migraine, paroxysmal hemicrania, SUNCT/SUNA, trigeminal neuralgia, sinusitis, acute angle-closure glaucoma, carotid dissection) and explains why cluster headache best fits.
Meets communication milestone: explains the diagnosis using the terms 'cluster headache' and 'familial' in patient-facing language.

Meets communication milestone: explains first-line acute treatments and uses the terms 'oxygen' and 'sumatriptan' (even if not prescribing at L0), including that oxygen is high-flow via nonrebreather and sumatriptan is typically subcutaneous/intranasal for rapid relief.

Performs required safety checks conceptually before any prescribing is discussed: asks about medication allergies (non-critical) and explicitly checks key contraindications and drug interactions for triptans (critical), such as ischemic heart disease, uncontrolled hypertension, stroke/TIA, hemiplegic/basilar migraine, severe hepatic impairment, recent ergotamine/other triptan use, and MAOI use.
Provides safety-net advice: seek urgent care for new neurologic symptoms, thunderclap onset, vision loss, severe eye pain/redness, or atypical/worsening pattern.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "nephritis" and explicitly links urinalysis findings to inflammation of the kidneys.
Mentions "RBC casts" as a key finding supporting a glomerular source of hematuria (glomerulonephritis/nephritic syndrome).
Explains the treatment plan and explicitly includes "steroids" as part of therapy (when appropriate for suspected immune-mediated nephritis).
Explicitly instructs the patient to "avoid NSAIDs" due to risk of worsening kidney injury and fluid retention.
Performs an allergy check before prescribing and does not prescribe a medication to which the patient is allergic (critical safety check).

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing (e.g., uncontrolled infection, poorly controlled diabetes, GI bleeding risk, severe hypertension, pregnancy considerations, active TB exposure) and adjusts/withholds steroids if contraindicated (critical safety check).
Considers drug-drug interactions before prescribing (non-critical), especially with anticoagulants, diabetes medications, and immunosuppressants, and provides mitigation/monitoring if relevant.
Uses shared decision-making: discusses expected benefits, risks, and monitoring needs of steroids and confirms patient understanding.
Provides red-flag return precautions for worsening renal function (oliguria/anuria), pulmonary edema/shortness of breath, severe hypertension symptoms, or systemic infection signs.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose nephritis (glomerulonephritis suspected) and start steroid therapy with renal-safety counseling, after required safety checks.
must_collect: blood pressure elevation symptoms or measured hypertension
baseline kidney function context (history of CKD or recent creatinine if known)
contraindications to steroids (active infection, uncontrolled diabetes, GI bleed history, pregnancy status as applicable)
current medication list for interaction screening
steps: Explain that findings are consistent with nephritis and that RBC casts suggest kidney (glomerular) bleeding/inflammation rather than lower urinary tract bleeding.
State the treatment approach includes steroids (if immune-mediated nephritis suspected and no contraindications), plus supportive measures and avoidance of nephrotoxins.
Complete allergy_check before prescribing.
Complete contraindication_check before prescribing steroids.
Optionally perform drug_interaction_check and plan monitoring if interactions exist.
Record diagnosis: nephritis (glomerulonephritis suspected).
Prescribe steroid regimen (e.g., prednisone per protocol/weight-based dosing) with counseling and monitoring advice.
Counsel to avoid NSAIDs and to use safer alternatives for pain/fever as appropriate (e.g., acetaminophen within safe limits).
Provide clear return precautions for worsening symptoms or signs of renal failure/infection.

acceptable_variants.description: Steroid-sparing initial management when steroids are contraindicated or infection is suspected, while still documenting nephritis and avoiding NSAIDs; may prescribe supportive medications instead of steroids.
steps: Explain nephritis diagnosis and significance of RBC casts.
Identify a steroid contraindication (e.g., active infection concern) and explain why steroids are deferred.
Record diagnosis: nephritis (glomerulonephritis suspected).

Prescribe appropriate supportive therapy (e.g., antihypertensive if markedly elevated BP and clinically appropriate; diuretic for symptomatic edema if appropriate; gastroprotection if needed) after allergy/contraindication checks.
Counsel to avoid NSAIDs and provide return precautions and monitoring plan.

acceptable_variants.description: Alternative steroid selection (e.g., IV methylprednisolone if severe presentation) while maintaining required safety checks and communication milestones.
steps: Explain nephritis and RBC casts in patient-friendly terms.
Discuss severity features prompting parenteral steroids and risks/benefits.
Perform allergy and contraindication checks prior to prescribing.
Record diagnosis: nephritis (glomerulonephritis suspected).
Prescribe methylprednisolone (route/dose per severity protocol) with counseling, including avoiding NSAIDs and infection precautions.
